properties.name: First Evidence
properties.description: The oldest depiction of a lute-typed instrument. Found in the Southern Mesopotamia, Cylinder Seal of the Uruk Period, 3000 BCE (Late Uruk, Uruk III) (British Museum Online Collection, Inv. No: 141632).

properties.name: Akkadian Lute I
properties.description: One of the oldest lute player depiction. Dates back to 2400-2200 BCE and said to be a product of Akkadian (Ancient Mesopotamian) (British Museum Online Collection, Inv. No: 28806)
properties.citation:
R. Dumbrill "Musical_Scenes_and_Instruments_on_seals.".; Bibliography: Boehmer, R.M., Die Entwicklung der Glyptik wahrend der Akkad-Zeit, 4, Berlin, (1965); Collon, D., Catalogue

Museum Publications, (1982); Rimmer, J., Ancient musical instruments of Western Asia, London, British Museum, (1969).
Fig. 82. BM 28806.

properties.name: Akkadian Lute II
properties.description: Cylinder Seal of the Akkadian Period, 2500-2000 BCE. (89096)

Inscription translation: PN, singer.

properties.name: Shepherd I
properties.description: Iraq, Shepherd playing a long-necked stringed instrument, 2025-1763 BCE, plates probably from the same mold as Shepherd II. (The Penn Museum Online Collection, Inv. Nos. L-29-303).
properties.citation: Penn Museum.; Polin, Claire C. Gifts of Jubal: Musical Instruments from the Ancient East. Philadelphia: The University Museum, 07 Apr 1954. See: figure VIII

properties.name: Shepherd II
properties.description: Iraq, Shepherd playing a long-necked stringed instrument, 2025-1763 BCE, plates probably from the same mold as Shepherd I. (The Penn Museum Online Collection, Inv. Nos. 53-11-90).

properties.name: A Possible Tuning Peg Example
properties.description: The long necked lute example on this stamped terracotta has a possibility of showing an evidence for the earliest "peg" depiction. Image is restricted and is "not for reproduction" (Stony Brook University, Academic Commons, Archive of Mesopotamian Archaeological Reports - AMAR).
properties.citation:

properties.name: Seven Warriors
properties.description: “Unfinished” Kudurru, Kassite period, attributed to the reign of Melishipak, 1186–1172 B.C.E., found in Susa, where it had been taken as war booty in the 12th century B.C.E. (Louvre, Paris; photo: Steven Zucker [https://flic.kr/p/osBGkR] , CC BY-NC-SA 2.0). Detail of one of seven warrior-lutenists, register 2/4 of carved limestone kudurru. Kassite period, Reign of Melišippak II, c. 1186-1172 BC.

properties.name: Central Asia Evidences I
properties.description: Three musicians (a drummer, a lutenist and a harper) bordered by acanthus leaves, from the Airtam Frieze, an artwork in the Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg, Russia. The artwork was originally part of the entrance to a Buddhist temple.

properties.name: First Evidence From Anatolia
properties.description: One of the oldest pieces of evidence regarding bağlama-type instruments found in Anatolia is a painted pottery fragment discovered in Samsat/Adıyaman (Dinçol 2003: 30). It dates back to the Old Hittite State period, i.e., 1650-1620 BC (Sipahi 2004: 30-31).

properties.name: İnandık Vase
properties.description: The vase unearthed at İnandık Tepe, dating to approximately 1600 BC, depicts two separate lute-type instrument players (Dinçol 2003: 30-31). The İnandık Vase, one of the famous examples of Hittite depiction and musical art, plays an important role as an indicator for the traces of the lute in Anatolia and among the Hittites.

properties.name: Alacahöyük Orthostat
properties.description: The wall relief / orthostat found in Alacahöyük, Çorum, is noteworthy for its depiction of a lute-type instrument with tassels and frets. Its body form differs from that of contemporary tanbura-type instruments, exhibiting a more segmented structure.

properties.name: Hittite Funeral Rituals Texts
properties.description: It is said that the Hittites had the oldest indigenous writing culture in Anatolia (Hawkins 1986: 363-364). This makes them the most important source of written information about Ancient Anatolia. Hittite cuneiform tablets show that the Hittites placed particular importance on the performance of musical instruments in birth and death rituals, healing and worship rituals, and ceremonies and festivals such as weddings and victories (Hitit Portalı: Myths – Rituals; Masalcı Şahin 2018: 27-39). It was initially suggested that the Sumerian word «(GIŠ2)ŠÀ.A.TAR», used in the geography of ancient Mesopotamia, referred to a stringed musical instrument (Masalcı Şahin 2016: 83). Later, this word, which is encountered in Hittite cuneiform tablets with its new reading as «TIBULA», is thought by many researchers to describe a lute-type instrument (Masalcı Şahin 2016: 86). The instrument referred to as «TIBULA», which is thought to be a lute-type instrument, is written to have accompanied the singer in the Hittite text titled «Death Ritual Day 1-2» (CTH 450.1.1.1, 180’.. Line, Kape?u?, Hethiter.net, online). In this respect, it is reminiscent of the ritual of playing the saz (a stringed instrument) after the death of a person in the Turkish minstrel tradition (Masalcı Şahin 2018: 138, 139). The word «TIBULA» also appears in the text related to the KI.LAM festival: Kbo X 24 / Masalcı Şahin 2018: 266

properties.name: Karkamış/Kargamış Orthostat (Procession)
properties.description: The Karkamış wall relief depicts a dancer and musicians, and shows the playing of a tasseled lute-like instrument. This provides evidence of the playing of lute-type instruments during the Late Hittite period.
